title: An epigenetic biomarker of aging for lifespan and healthspan
finding: PhenoAge derived from 9 clinical chemistry biomarkers captures mortality risk and aging-related conditions. Validated in NHANES III (n=9,926) and InCHIANTI. PhenoAge acceleration predicts all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and physical functioning decline.
text_chunk: Levine et al. 2018 developed PhenoAge using 9 blood biomarkers (albumin, creatinine, glucose, hs-CRP, lymphocyte %, MCV, RDW, alkaline phosphatase, WBC) plus chronological age. PhenoAge acceleration >5 years associated with 2.3x mortality risk. Validated across multiple cohorts.

title: DNA methylation GrimAge strongly predicts lifespan and healthspan
finding: GrimAge, based on DNA methylation surrogates of 7 plasma proteins and smoking pack-years, is the strongest epigenetic predictor of mortality. GrimAge acceleration predicts time-to-death, cancer, CHD, and chronic disease.
text_chunk: Lu et al. 2019 developed GrimAge using DNAm surrogates of: adrenomedullin, beta-2 microglobulin, cystatin C, GDF-15, leptin, PAI-1, TIMP-1, plus smoking pack-years. GrimAge is the strongest mortality predictor among epigenetic clocks. Each year of GrimAge acceleration increases mortality risk ~8%.

title: DNA methylation age of human tissues and cell types
finding: First multi-tissue epigenetic clock based on 353 CpG sites. Horvath clock measures intrinsic epigenetic aging independent of blood cell composition. Applicable across all tissues and cell types.
text_chunk: Horvath 2013 developed the first pan-tissue epigenetic clock using 353 CpG sites. Measures intrinsic biological age independent of blood cell composition. Deviation from chronological age predicts mortality. Foundation for all subsequent epigenetic clock research.

title: Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin
finding: Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP): intensive lifestyle intervention reduced T2D incidence by 58% vs placebo in high-risk prediabetic adults (n=3,234). Metformin reduced incidence by 31%. Lifestyle was superior to metformin across all subgroups.
text_chunk: DPP trial (NEJM 2002, n=3,234): intensive lifestyle (7% weight loss + 150 min/week exercise) reduced T2D incidence 58% vs placebo. Metformin 850mg BID reduced incidence 31%. NNT=6.9 for lifestyle over 2.8 years. Lifestyle benefit sustained at 15-year follow-up (DPP Outcomes Study).

title: Evolocumab and Clinical Outcomes in Patients with Cardiovascular Disease
finding: FOURIER trial (n=27,564): evolocumab (PCSK9 inhibitor) reduced LDL-C by 59% (from 92 to 30 mg/dL) and reduced major CV events by 15% (HR 0.85) over 2.2 years in statin-treated patients with established ASCVD.
text_chunk: FOURIER trial (NEJM 2017, n=27,564): PCSK9 inhibitor evolocumab reduced LDL-C 59% and CV events 15% (HR 0.85) in patients already on statins. Established that very low LDL (<20 mg/dL) is safe. Validated PCSK9 loss-of-function genetic evidence (rs11591147) as therapeutic target.

title: Alirocumab and Cardiovascular Outcomes after Acute Coronary Syndrome
finding: ODYSSEY Outcomes (n=18,924): alirocumab reduced MACE by 15% (HR 0.85) in post-ACS patients. Greatest benefit in those with LDL >=100 mg/dL at baseline. Also showed trend toward reduced all-cause mortality.
text_chunk: ODYSSEY Outcomes (NEJM 2018, n=18,924): alirocumab (PCSK9i) reduced MACE 15% in post-ACS patients. All-cause mortality trend favoring alirocumab. Greatest benefit with baseline LDL >=100 mg/dL. Supports aggressive LDL lowering post-ACS.

title: Rosuvastatin to prevent vascular events in men and women with elevated C-reactive protein
finding: JUPITER trial (n=17,802): rosuvastatin 20mg reduced major CV events by 44% in patients with LDL <130 mg/dL but hs-CRP >2 mg/L. Established hs-CRP as a valid target for CV risk reduction. Trial stopped early for overwhelming efficacy.
text_chunk: JUPITER trial (NEJM 2008, n=17,802): rosuvastatin reduced CV events 44% in patients with 'normal' LDL but elevated hs-CRP (>2 mg/L). Established residual inflammatory risk as a therapeutic target. hs-CRP >2 mg/L identifies statin candidates even with LDL <130. Stopped early for efficacy.

title: Antiinflammatory Therapy with Canakinumab for Atherosclerotic Disease
finding: CANTOS trial (n=10,061): canakinumab (IL-1beta antibody) reduced hs-CRP 26-41% and CV events 15% (HR 0.85) independent of lipid lowering. First proof that directly targeting inflammation reduces CV events.
text_chunk: CANTOS trial (NEJM 2017, n=10,061): canakinumab (anti-IL-1beta) reduced CV events 15% without changing LDL. Proved the inflammatory hypothesis of atherosclerosis. Patients achieving hs-CRP <2 mg/L had 25% CV reduction. IL-6/CRP pathway is a causal mediator of CV disease.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Low-Dose Colchicine after Myocardial Infarction
finding: COLCOT trial (n=4,745): low-dose colchicine 0.5mg daily reduced composite CV endpoint by 23% (HR 0.77) in post-MI patients. Established colchicine as an affordable anti-inflammatory strategy for CV risk reduction.
text_chunk: COLCOT trial (NEJM 2019, n=4,745): colchicine 0.5mg daily post-MI reduced CV events 23% (HR 0.77). Affordable anti-inflammatory therapy for residual CV risk. Supports targeting the IL-1beta/IL-6/CRP inflammatory cascade. Now guideline-recommended post-MI.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Icosapent Ethyl for Hypertriglyceridemia
finding: REDUCE-IT (n=8,179): icosapent ethyl (EPA) 4g/day reduced major CV events by 25% (HR 0.75) in statin-treated patients with elevated triglycerides. Benefit beyond triglyceride lowering, likely through anti-inflammatory mechanisms.
text_chunk: REDUCE-IT (NEJM 2019, n=8,179): icosapent ethyl (pure EPA) 4g/day reduced CV events 25% (HR 0.75) in patients with TG 135-499 on statins. Benefit beyond TG lowering - EPA has anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, and membrane-stabilizing effects. FADS1 CC carriers may have greater benefit from direct EPA supplementation.

title: Effect of High-Dose Omega-3 Fatty Acids vs Corn Oil on Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events
finding: STRENGTH trial (n=13,078): carboxylic acid omega-3 (EPA+DHA) did not reduce CV events vs corn oil. Contradicted REDUCE-IT, suggesting EPA-only formulation may be superior to EPA+DHA combinations for CV benefit.
text_chunk: STRENGTH trial (JAMA 2020, n=13,078): mixed EPA+DHA (carboxylic acid formulation) showed no CV benefit vs corn oil. Contrasts with REDUCE-IT (EPA-only). Suggests EPA-specific anti-inflammatory mechanism, or REDUCE-IT mineral oil placebo inflated benefit. Current evidence favors EPA-only over EPA+DHA for CV risk.

title: Marine n-3 Fatty Acids and Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease and Cancer
finding: VITAL trial (n=25,871): omega-3 1g/day did not reduce major CV events or cancer in primary prevention. However, post-hoc analysis showed 28% reduction in MI and benefit in African Americans.
text_chunk: VITAL trial (NEJM 2019, n=25,871): standard-dose omega-3 (1g/day) no overall CV benefit in primary prevention. Post-hoc: 28% MI reduction, stronger in African Americans and those with low fish intake. Suggests higher doses (4g as in REDUCE-IT) needed for CV benefit. Low dose insufficient.

title: Genetic variation in PNPLA3 confers susceptibility to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
finding: Romeo et al. identified PNPLA3 rs738409 (I148M) as the strongest genetic risk factor for NAFLD across multiple ethnic groups. GG homozygotes had 73% more hepatic fat than CC. Dallas Heart Study (n=2,111).
text_chunk: Romeo et al. 2008 (Nature Genetics): GWAS identified PNPLA3 I148M (rs738409) as strongest NAFLD risk variant. GG homozygotes: 73% more hepatic fat, significantly higher ALT. Replicated across all ethnic groups. Hispanic Americans have highest G allele frequency, explaining ethnic differences in NAFLD prevalence.

title: A Protein-Truncating HSD17B13 Variant and Protection from Chronic Liver Disease
finding: Abul-Husn et al. discovered HSD17B13 rs72613567 splice variant as protective against chronic liver disease. Each TA allele: 17% lower NASH risk, 30% lower cirrhosis risk, 49% lower HCC risk. n=46,544.
text_chunk: Abul-Husn et al. 2018 (NEJM, n=46,544): HSD17B13 rs72613567 TA insertion is protective against chronic liver disease. Per-allele: ALT -4 U/L, NASH risk -17%, cirrhosis -30%, HCC -49%. Protective even in PNPLA3 GG carriers. Active drug target (RNAi therapies in development).

title: SLCO1B1 variants and statin-induced myopathy
finding: SEARCH study (n=12,064): SLCO1B1 rs4149056 CC (Val174Ala) conferred 17x increased risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy. Established SLCO1B1 genotyping as standard of care before high-dose statin initiation.
text_chunk: SEARCH study (NEJM 2008, n=12,064): SLCO1B1 *5/*5 (rs4149056 CC) confers 17x risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy at 80mg dose. 4.5x risk in heterozygotes. Led to FDA recommendation against simvastatin 80mg. CPIC guideline: rosuvastatin preferred for SLCO1B1 *5 carriers.

title: CPIC Guideline for Pharmacogenetics-Guided Warfarin Dosing: 2017 Update
finding: CPIC guidelines provide evidence-based recommendations for CYP2D6 phenotype-guided prescribing of codeine, tramadol, tamoxifen, TCAs, atomoxetine, ondansetron, and beta-blockers. Level 1A evidence for most drug-gene pairs.
text_chunk: CPIC CYP2D6 guidelines cover codeine/tramadol (poor: avoid, ultra-rapid: avoid), tamoxifen (poor: use AI), TCAs (poor: 50% dose reduction), atomoxetine (poor: 50-75% reduction), beta-blockers (poor: reduce metoprolol). Activity Score system standardizes phenotype assignment across star alleles.

title: CPIC Guideline for CYP2C19 and Clopidogrel Therapy: 2022 Update
finding: Updated CPIC guideline for CYP2C19-guided clopidogrel therapy. Poor and intermediate metabolizers should receive alternative antiplatelet (prasugrel, ticagrelor). Evidence from multiple RCTs and meta-analyses.
text_chunk: CPIC CYP2C19 2022 update: poor metabolizers (*2/*2, *2/*3, *3/*3) should avoid clopidogrel - use prasugrel or ticagrelor. Intermediate metabolizers (*1/*2): consider alternative, especially for ACS/PCI. TAILOR-PCI and POPular Genetics trials support genotype-guided antiplatelet selection.

title: CPIC Guideline for Pharmacogenetics-Guided Warfarin Dosing
finding: CPIC warfarin guideline: VKORC1 rs9923231 and CYP2C9 genotype explain ~40% of warfarin dose variability. Pharmacogenomic dosing algorithms reduce time to therapeutic INR and bleeding events.
text_chunk: CPIC Warfarin Guideline 2017: VKORC1 + CYP2C9 genotyping recommended before warfarin initiation. VKORC1 AA: reduce dose ~50%. CYP2C9 *2/*3: reduce dose ~50-70%. Combined VKORC1 AA + CYP2C9 *2/*3 may need <1 mg/day. EU-PACT and COAG trials evaluated clinical implementation.

title: CPIC Guidelines for HLA-B*5701 Genotype and Abacavir
finding: HLA-B*57:01 screening before abacavir is mandatory (FDA, EMA). Eliminates immunologically confirmed hypersensitivity reactions. 100% negative predictive value. Most successful PGx implementation globally.
text_chunk: CPIC HLA-B*57:01 Guideline: mandatory screening before abacavir. Positive result: CONTRAINDICATED (50% HSR risk). Most successful pharmacogenomics implementation - eliminated abacavir hypersensitivity in clinical practice. PREDICT-1 trial demonstrated clinical utility. 100% NPV.

title: CPIC Guideline for Thiopurines and TPMT/NUDT15
finding: CPIC thiopurine guideline: TPMT and NUDT15 genotyping recommended before azathioprine/6-MP/6-TG. Deficient: 10% dose or avoid. Intermediate: 30-70% dose. Prevents potentially fatal myelosuppression.
text_chunk: CPIC Thiopurine Guideline 2019: TPMT *3A/*3A (deficient): use 10% of standard thiopurine dose or avoid. *1/*3A (intermediate): 30-70% dose. NUDT15 *3/*3: same as TPMT deficient. Both genes should be tested. Prevents fatal myelosuppression in ALL maintenance and IBD treatment.

title: Prediction of coronary heart disease using risk factor categories
finding: Framingham Heart Study risk algorithm: uses age, sex, total cholesterol, HDL-C, systolic BP, smoking, diabetes to predict 10-year CHD risk. Foundation for all subsequent cardiovascular risk calculators. Continuous follow-up since 1948.
text_chunk: Framingham Risk Score (Circulation 1998): landmark risk prediction model using age, sex, cholesterol, HDL, SBP, smoking, diabetes. Foundation for ACC/AHA Pooled Cohort Equations (PCE). Framingham Heart Study (since 1948) identified most major CV risk factors including hypertension, cholesterol, smoking, diabetes.

title: Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease with a Mediterranean Diet Supplemented with Extra-Virgin Olive Oil or Nuts
finding: PREDIMED trial (n=7,447): Mediterranean diet supplemented with EVOO or nuts reduced major CV events by ~30% vs low-fat diet. No calorie restriction needed. Benefits seen in high CV risk population.
text_chunk: PREDIMED trial (NEJM 2018 republished, n=7,447): Mediterranean diet + EVOO or nuts reduced CV events ~30% vs low-fat diet. No calorie restriction. Benefits for stroke prevention particularly strong. Mediterranean diet also reduces NAFLD progression. Foundation for dietary recommendations across disease trajectories.

title: Intensive blood-glucose control with sulphonylureas or insulin compared with conventional treatment and risk of complications in patients with type 2 diabetes
finding: UKPDS (n=3,867): intensive glucose control (HbA1c 7.0% vs 7.9%) reduced microvascular complications by 25% but no significant macrovascular benefit at 10 years. Legacy effect: 10-year post-trial follow-up showed emerging MI reduction.
text_chunk: UKPDS (Lancet 1998, n=3,867): intensive glucose control (HbA1c 7.0%) reduced microvascular complications 25%. No initial macrovascular benefit. UKPDS post-trial monitoring (2008): legacy effect - MI reduction 15% (p=0.01) emerged after 10 additional years. Early intensive control has lasting benefits.

title: Empagliflozin, Cardiovascular Outcomes, and Mortality in Type 2 Diabetes
finding: EMPA-REG OUTCOME (n=7,020): empagliflozin reduced CV death 38% (HR 0.62), all-cause mortality 32%, and HF hospitalization 35% in T2D with established CVD. Paradigm-changing trial for SGLT2 inhibitors.
text_chunk: EMPA-REG OUTCOME (NEJM 2015, n=7,020): empagliflozin reduced CV death 38% and HF hospitalization 35% in T2D + CVD. Benefits appeared within weeks (hemodynamic, not glycemic mechanism). Led to SGLT2i becoming cornerstone of T2D treatment with CV/renal comorbidity. Dapagliflozin and canagliflozin showed similar benefits.

title: Liraglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes
finding: LEADER trial (n=9,340): liraglutide reduced MACE 13% (HR 0.87), CV death 22%, and all-cause mortality 15% in T2D with high CV risk. Established GLP-1 RA class for CV risk reduction.
text_chunk: LEADER trial (NEJM 2016, n=9,340): liraglutide reduced MACE 13%, CV death 22% in high-risk T2D. Semaglutide (SUSTAIN-6, SELECT trials) showed similar or greater CV benefit. GLP-1 RA now guideline-recommended for T2D + CVD. Additional benefits: weight loss, NASH resolution, renal protection.

title: Effects of Intensive Glucose Lowering in Type 2 Diabetes
finding: ACCORD trial (n=10,251): intensive glucose lowering (HbA1c <6.0%) increased mortality vs standard (7.0-7.9%) in high-risk T2D. Intensive arm stopped early. Caution against over-aggressive glucose targets in established CVD.
text_chunk: ACCORD trial (NEJM 2008, n=10,251): targeting HbA1c <6.0% increased mortality 22% vs standard target in high-risk T2D. Intensive arm stopped early. Hypoglycemia likely contributed. Current guidance: HbA1c <7% for most, <6.5% only if achievable without hypoglycemia. Individualize targets.

title: A Randomized Trial of Intensive versus Standard Blood-Pressure Control
finding: SPRINT trial (n=9,361): intensive BP target (<120 mmHg systolic) reduced CV events 25% and all-cause mortality 27% vs standard (<140 mmHg) in high-risk non-diabetic adults. Led to updated BP guidelines.
text_chunk: SPRINT trial (NEJM 2015, n=9,361): intensive SBP target <120 mmHg reduced CV events 25% and mortality 27% vs <140 mmHg. Stopped early for benefit. Led to ACC/AHA 2017 guidelines lowering HTN threshold to 130/80. More aggressive targets particularly beneficial for high-risk patients.

title: CPIC Guideline for Dihydropyrimidine Dehydrogenase Genotype and Fluoropyrimidine Dosing
finding: CPIC DPYD guideline: DPYD-deficient patients should avoid 5-FU and capecitabine or use greatly reduced doses. >10% mortality risk with standard dosing in DPYD-deficient patients. Pre-treatment genotyping now mandatory in EU.
text_chunk: CPIC DPYD Guideline: DPYD *2A homozygous: AVOID 5-FU/capecitabine (>10% mortality at standard dose). Heterozygous: 50% dose reduction. DPYD genotyping mandatory in EU before fluoropyrimidine therapy. Critical for GI cancer treatment planning. 3-5% of population carries at least one reduced-function allele.

title: PNPLA3 I148M polymorphism and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease: meta-analysis
finding: Meta-analysis of 24 studies: PNPLA3 rs738409 GG vs CC: OR 3.24 for NAFLD, 5.30 for NASH, 3.25 for fibrosis, 1.67 for HCC. Effect modified by alcohol intake and BMI.
text_chunk: PNPLA3 meta-analysis (2014, 24 studies): GG vs CC: NAFLD OR 3.24, NASH OR 5.30, fibrosis OR 3.25, HCC OR 1.67. Alcohol amplifies PNPLA3 risk synergistically. BMI modifies effect - lean NAFLD in GG carriers occurs but is less common. Strongest genetic effect on liver disease risk.

title: Selenium Supplementation for Hashimoto Thyroiditis: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
finding: Meta-analysis: selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day selenomethionine) reduced TPO antibodies by ~20-40% in Hashimoto thyroiditis patients over 3-12 months. Modest improvement in thyroid function.
text_chunk: Selenium meta-analysis for Hashimoto: 200 mcg/day selenomethionine reduces TPO antibodies 20-40% over 3-12 months. May slow progression from subclinical to overt hypothyroidism. Selenomethionine preferred over sodium selenite. Optimal selenium status: 100-130 mcg/L. Supports deiodinase function for T4-to-T3 conversion.

title: Combined analysis of fourteen cohorts confirms a common risk variant for hypothyroidism
finding: Panicker et al. 2009: DIO2 Thr92Ala variant associated with impaired psychological well-being on levothyroxine monotherapy. Carriers prefer combination T4/T3 therapy. Meta-analysis of 14 cohorts.
text_chunk: Panicker et al. 2009: DIO2 Thr92Ala (rs225014) AA carriers on levothyroxine monotherapy report worse psychological well-being and prefer combination T4/T3 therapy in blinded crossover design. Supports genotype-guided thyroid hormone replacement. ~16% of population is AA homozygous.

title: MTHFR C677T polymorphism and risk of coronary heart disease: A meta-analysis
finding: Meta-analysis of 40 studies: MTHFR C677T TT genotype associated with 16% increased CHD risk (OR 1.16). Mediated through elevated homocysteine. Greater effect in low folate regions.
text_chunk: MTHFR C677T meta-analysis (40 studies): TT genotype increases CHD risk 16% (OR 1.16) mediated through homocysteine elevation. Effect stronger in populations with low folate fortification. L-methylfolate supplementation for MTHFR TT reduces homocysteine and may reduce CV risk, though RCTs of homocysteine-lowering show mixed results.

title: A null mutation in SERPINE1 protects against biological aging in humans
finding: Khan et al. 2017: heterozygous SERPINE1 null mutation in Old Order Amish associated with longer telomeres (+10%), lower fasting insulin, and reduced metabolic disease. Provides human evidence for PAI-1 as aging target.
text_chunk: Khan et al. 2017 (Science Medicine): SERPINE1 null mutation in Amish community: heterozygotes have 10% longer telomeres, lower PAI-1, lower fasting insulin, and trend toward longer lifespan. PAI-1 is a GrimAge component. Validates PAI-1 as a therapeutic target for aging and metabolic disease.

title: A Common Variant in the FTO Gene Is Associated with Body Mass Index and Predisposes to Childhood and Adult Obesity
finding: Frayling et al. 2007: FTO rs9939609 first GWAS hit for obesity. Each A allele increases BMI 0.36 kg/m2. AA homozygotes have 1.67x odds of obesity. Replicated across all populations.
text_chunk: FTO rs9939609 (Science 2007): first obesity GWAS hit. Each A allele: +0.36 kg/m2 BMI, 1.3x obesity risk. AA homozygotes: 1.67x obesity risk, ~3 kg heavier. FTO regulates IRX3/IRX5 in adipocyte precursors, affecting thermogenesis. Lifestyle intervention can override genetic predisposition.

title: Sequence variations in PCSK9, low LDL, and protection against coronary heart disease
finding: Cohen et al. 2006: PCSK9 loss-of-function variants (including R46L rs11591147) reduce LDL-C 15-28% and CHD risk 47-88%. Lifelong exposure to lower LDL has greater CV benefit per mg/dL than statin-achieved LDL reduction.
text_chunk: Cohen et al. 2006 (NEJM): PCSK9 loss-of-function variants provide Mendelian randomization proof that LDL is causal for CHD. R46L (rs11591147 T): LDL -15%, CHD risk -47%. Y142X and C679X: LDL -28%, CHD risk -88%. Lifelong genetic LDL reduction ~3x more effective per mg/dL than statin therapy started in middle age.

title: A novel MHC class I-like gene is mutated in patients with hereditary haemochromatosis
finding: Feder et al. 1996: discovery of HFE gene. C282Y (rs1800562) mutation found in 85% of hereditary hemochromatosis patients. H63D (rs1799945) identified as modifier. Enabled genetic screening for iron overload.
text_chunk: Feder et al. 1996 (Nature Genetics): HFE gene discovery. C282Y homozygosity in 85% of hemochromatosis patients. Prevalence: ~1 in 200 Northern Europeans are C282Y/C282Y. H63D is a common modifier. HFE genotyping when TSAT >45% is standard of care. Phlebotomy prevents organ damage if started early.

title: Vitamin E and the Risk of Prostate Cancer: SELECT
finding: SELECT trial (n=35,533): vitamin E supplementation (400 IU/day) did not prevent cancer and increased prostate cancer risk by 17% with long-term follow-up. Selenium supplementation also showed no benefit.
text_chunk: SELECT trial (JAMA 2009, n=35,533): vitamin E 400 IU/day increased prostate cancer risk 17% in healthy men. Selenium supplementation (L-selenomethionine 200 mcg) showed no cancer prevention benefit. Caution against high-dose antioxidant supplementation without evidence. Individual selenium status may modify response.

title: PhenoAge validation in NHANES III
finding: PhenoAge validated in NHANES III (n=9,926): PhenoAge acceleration >5 years associated with 2.3x mortality risk. PhenoAge outperformed Horvath clock for mortality prediction. 9 clinical biomarkers are routinely available.
text_chunk: PhenoAge in NHANES III (n=9,926): 9 biomarker composite age predicts mortality better than any individual biomarker. Components: albumin (-0.0336), creatinine (0.0095, log), glucose (0.00188, log), hs-CRP (0.0167, log), lymphocyte % (-0.0120), MCV (0.0268), RDW (0.0952), ALP (0.0203, log), WBC (0.0954).

title: Effects of age, sex, and ethnicity on the association between apolipoprotein E genotype and Alzheimer disease
finding: Meta-analysis: APOE E4 increases Alzheimer disease risk 3.2x (heterozygous) to 14.9x (homozygous). E2 is protective (OR 0.6). Effect strongest in ages 55-65, attenuating after 75. Women have higher risk per E4 allele.
text_chunk: APOE-AD meta-analysis: E4 heterozygous OR 3.2, E4/E4 OR 14.9 for Alzheimer disease. E2 protective (OR 0.6). 40-65% of AD patients carry at least one E4 allele. E4 promotes amyloid-beta aggregation and impairs clearance. APOE genotyping increasingly used in AD risk stratification and clinical trial enrollment.

title: HSD17B13 as a therapeutic target for NASH
finding: HSD17B13 inhibition validated as therapeutic target for NASH through genetic evidence. ARO-HSD17B13 (RNAi therapy) and other approaches in clinical trials. Loss-of-function mutations provide ~50% HCC risk reduction.
text_chunk: HSD17B13 as therapeutic target: genetic evidence (rs72613567 TA) shows liver protection across the NAFLD spectrum. ARO-HSD (Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals) RNAi therapy in Phase 1/2 trials. Genetic loss-of-function provides 17% NASH reduction, 30% cirrhosis reduction, 49% HCC reduction per TA allele. First genetically-validated liver drug target.

title: COMT Val158Met polymorphism and cognition
finding: Meta-analysis of COMT Val158Met: Met/Met (low activity) associated with better executive function and working memory but higher anxiety and stress sensitivity. Val/Val (high activity) associated with stress resilience but reduced baseline cognitive performance.
text_chunk: COMT Val158Met meta-analysis: Met/Met (AA): better prefrontal cognition, higher dopamine, but increased anxiety sensitivity. Val/Val (GG): stress resilient, lower dopamine, better under pressure. Context-dependent effects - no universally 'better' allele. Affects pain sensitivity, estrogen metabolism, and drug responses.

title: Variant of transcription factor 7-like 2 (TCF7L2) gene confers risk of type 2 diabetes
finding: Grant et al. 2006: TCF7L2 rs7903146 identified as strongest common T2D risk variant. Each T allele increases risk 1.45x. Population attributable risk ~21%. Replicated across all ethnic groups.
text_chunk: TCF7L2 rs7903146 (Nature Genetics 2006): strongest common T2D risk variant. Per T allele OR 1.45. TT homozygotes: OR 2.1. Population attributable risk ~21%. Impairs incretin signaling and beta-cell function via Wnt pathway. TCF7L2 TT carriers respond better to lifestyle intervention than metformin (DPP subanalysis).

title: GrimAge component surrogates and mortality prediction
finding: GrimAge includes DNAm surrogates for 7 plasma proteins: adrenomedullin, beta-2 microglobulin, cystatin C, GDF-15, leptin, PAI-1, TIMP-1, plus smoking pack-years. Each component independently predicts mortality.
text_chunk: GrimAge components (Lu 2019): DNAm surrogates for adrenomedullin (vascular), beta-2 microglobulin (immune/renal), cystatin C (renal), GDF-15 (mitochondrial stress), leptin (metabolic), PAI-1 (thrombosis/senescence), TIMP-1 (fibrosis), plus smoking. Each independently predicts mortality. Composite GrimAge is strongest mortality predictor.

title: Semaglutide for NASH Resolution
finding: Semaglutide Phase 2b/3 trials showed 59% NASH resolution without worsening fibrosis vs 17% placebo. Also improved fibrosis by at least one stage in 43% vs 33%. GLP-1 RA emerging as leading NASH therapy.
text_chunk: Semaglutide for NASH: Phase 3 showed 59% NASH resolution vs 17% placebo (p<0.001). Fibrosis improvement in 43% vs 33%. Weight loss ~13% with 2.4mg weekly. GLP-1 RA addresses both metabolic and hepatic components of NAFLD/NASH. PNPLA3 GG carriers with NASH may particularly benefit given metabolic-genetic synergy.

title: Effect of Genotype-Guided Oral P2Y12 Inhibitor Selection vs Conventional Clopidogrel Therapy on Ischemic Outcomes After Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
finding: TAILOR-PCI (n=5,302): CYP2C19 genotype-guided antiplatelet selection (ticagrelor for carriers) vs universal clopidogrel reduced ischemic events in loss-of-function carriers (HR 0.66) but not in overall population.
text_chunk: TAILOR-PCI (JAMA 2020, n=5,302): CYP2C19 genotype-guided strategy reduced CV events 34% (HR 0.66) in loss-of-function allele carriers. Validates genotype-guided antiplatelet selection post-PCI. POPular Genetics trial confirmed: genotype-guided approach is non-inferior with less bleeding.

title: Epigenetic age acceleration and mortality: a meta-analysis
finding: Meta-analysis of 13 cohorts (n=13,089): each year of epigenetic age acceleration associated with 4% increased mortality (HR 1.04). Consistent across Horvath, Hannum, PhenoAge, and GrimAge clocks.
text_chunk: Epigenetic age acceleration meta-analysis (13 cohorts, n=13,089): each year of biological age acceleration increases mortality risk 4% (HR 1.04). GrimAge acceleration has strongest mortality association. Lifestyle factors (exercise, diet, sleep) can decelerate biological aging. Blood biomarker optimization may partially mediate this effect.

title: Klotho: a gene related to aging
finding: Klotho gene encodes anti-aging protein. Klotho-deficient mice show premature aging phenotype. Overexpression extends lifespan 20-30%. Soluble Klotho declines with age and CKD in humans.
text_chunk: Klotho (discovered 1997, Science): anti-aging protein. Mouse Klotho deficiency causes premature aging (shortened lifespan, atherosclerosis, osteoporosis, cognitive decline). Overexpression extends lifespan 20-30%. Soluble Klotho in humans: declines with age, low in CKD. KL-VS variant affects cognitive function. Exercise and vitamin D increase Klotho levels.

title: Average risks of breast and ovarian cancer associated with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations
finding: Meta-analysis: BRCA1 mutation carriers have 65% lifetime breast cancer risk and 39% ovarian cancer risk by age 70. BRCA2: 45% breast and 11% ovarian. Risk-reducing surgery dramatically reduces cancer-specific mortality.
text_chunk: BRCA1/2 cancer risk meta-analysis: BRCA1 carriers: 65% breast cancer by 70, 39% ovarian. BRCA2: 45% breast, 11% ovarian. Risk-reducing mastectomy reduces breast cancer risk >90%. Risk-reducing oophorectomy reduces ovarian cancer risk 80% and breast cancer 50%. PARP inhibitors (olaparib) for BRCA-associated cancers.

title: Berberine for metabolic health: systematic review
finding: Meta-analysis: berberine 500mg TID reduces HbA1c ~0.7%, fasting glucose ~15 mg/dL, LDL ~20 mg/dL, triglycerides ~35 mg/dL. Comparable to metformin for glucose lowering. AMPK activation mechanism.
text_chunk: Berberine meta-analysis: 500mg TID reduces HbA1c -0.7%, FPG -15 mg/dL, LDL -20 mg/dL, TG -35 mg/dL. Activates AMPK pathway similar to metformin. Useful adjunct for metabolic syndrome, prediabetes, and dyslipidemia. GI side effects (diarrhea) common. May be particularly useful for insulin-resistant phenotype.

title: NAD+ metabolism and its roles in cellular processes during aging
finding: NAD+ declines with age, impairing sirtuins and PARP activity. NMN and NR supplementation restore NAD+ levels in animal models. Human trials show NAD+ elevation but clinical outcomes data still emerging.
text_chunk: NAD+ decline is a hallmark of aging. NAD+ is essential for sirtuin activation (SIRT1-7), PARP DNA repair, and mitochondrial function. NMN and NR precursors restore NAD+ in animal models with healthspan benefits. Human trials (NR 300-1000mg, NMN 250-1200mg) show NAD+ elevation. Clinical outcome data still emerging.

title: VDR polymorphisms and vitamin D status
finding: VDR polymorphisms (FokI, BsmI, TaqI, ApaI) modulate vitamin D receptor signaling efficiency. FokI (rs2228570) affects VDR translation start site. Variant carriers may need higher 25-OH vitamin D levels for equivalent biological effect.
text_chunk: VDR polymorphisms affect vitamin D receptor efficiency. FokI rs2228570 CC: potentially reduced VDR activity, may need higher serum 25-OH-D (50-60 ng/mL). BsmI, TaqI, ApaI variants affect VDR expression levels. Genotype-guided vitamin D dosing optimizes calcium absorption, immune function, and bone health.

title: FADS genetic variants and omega-3 fatty acid metabolism
finding: Meta-analysis: FADS1/2 variants explain ~30% of variation in EPA/DHA levels and omega-3 index. FADS1 rs174546 C allele associated with lower delta-5 desaturase activity. Ancestral allele frequency varies dramatically by population.
text_chunk: FADS1/2 meta-analysis: explain ~30% of omega-3 fatty acid variation. FADS1 rs174546 CC: 25-40% lower EPA/DHA conversion from plant ALA. African-descent populations have higher T allele frequency (efficient converters). European-descent: higher C allele frequency (poor converters). Genotype-guided omega-3 supplementation recommended.

title: Sulforaphane and its effects on cancer, mortality, aging, and metabolic disease
finding: Sulforaphane (from broccoli sprouts) activates Nrf2 pathway, the master regulator of antioxidant defense. Reduces oxidative stress, inflammation, and has anti-cancer properties. Dose: 30-60mg sulforaphane equivalent daily.
text_chunk: Sulforaphane (broccoli sprout extract): potent Nrf2 activator, upregulating >200 cytoprotective genes. Reduces oxidative stress biomarkers, improves insulin sensitivity, and has anti-cancer properties. GSTM1 null genotype may enhance sulforaphane bioavailability. Dose: 30-60mg (equivalent to 100g broccoli sprouts). Myrosinase required for conversion.

title: GlyNAC supplementation and aging biomarkers
finding: GlyNAC (glycine + N-acetylcysteine) supplementation in older adults: restored glutathione levels, reduced oxidative stress, improved mitochondrial function, and reversed multiple aging biomarkers in RCT.
text_chunk: GlyNAC supplementation (glycine 1.33mmol/kg + NAC 0.81mmol/kg daily): RCT in older adults showed restored glutathione, reduced oxidative stress, improved mitochondrial function, reduced inflammation, improved insulin resistance, and reduced waist circumference. More effective than NAC alone for glutathione repletion.
